summarization.a: Model of a single hexagonal close-packed layer of CH4 molecules, with the H1–C bond constrained along the z-axis to represent the surface Al–CH3 bond.
summarization.b: First-principles (FP) computed energetics as a function of C–C distance, R, along with the optimized C–H bond lengths (excluding the z-oriented H1–C bond). The plot illustrates how steric strain affects both the energy and C–H bond distances at different CH3 surface coverages.

data_extraction.a:
| Model | Description |
|---|---|
| Close-packed CH₄ | H1–C constrained along z, representing the surface Al–CH₃ bond |

a.question: How does the C–C distance, R, affect the strain in the CH4 layer during ALD?
a.answer: As the C–C distance decreases below 3.8 Å, strain increases, leading to contraction of the C–H bonds and a higher energy penalty (ΔE). At R > 3.8 Å, there is minimal CH3–CH3 interaction, indicating negligible steric strain.

b.question: What structural representation is used to model the surface Al–CH3 bond?
b.answer: A single hexagonal close-packed layer of CH4 molecules is used, with the H1–C bond constrained along the z-axis to mimic the vertical orientation of the Al–CH3 bond on the surface.

d.question: Why is understanding the steric strain at different C–C distances important for ALD of Al2O3?
d.answer: Yes, it is important for controlling ALD surface chemistry.

c.question: At what C–C distance does CH3–CH3 interaction become negligible, and how does this compare to smaller distances?
c.answer: CH3–CH3 interaction becomes negligible at R > 3.8 Å, where ΔE < 0.2 eV and C–H bond lengths approach 1.10 Å, At smaller distances (R ≤ 3.0 Å), interaction is significant, ΔE increases (up to 1.8 eV), and C–H bonds contract to around 1.05 Å
